title: Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita, 2023
Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and vegetable oil availability for 2023, plotting GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis against vegetable oil supply in kilograms per year on the vertical axis. The data points are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The distribution shows a general positive association where higher GDP per capita coincides with higher vegetable oil supply, though the spread is wide. At the lower end of the economic scale, countries like the Democratic Republic of Congo and Niger sit below the 5 kg mark with GDPs under $2,000. In the middle range, nations such as Jordan, Belize, and Iraq cluster between 10 kg and 15 kg of oil supply with GDPs ranging from $10,000 to $20,000. High-income countries generally occupy the upper right quadrant; the United States and Canada show supplies between 20 kg and 30 kg with GDPs exceeding $50,000. Australia and Austria also appear in this high-supply, high-GDP band. However, the pattern is not uniform across all wealthy nations. Several European countries with GDPs over $50,000, including Denmark, Norway, and Iceland, display vegetable oil supplies closer to 10 kg or 15 kg, significantly lower than their North American counterparts. Conversely, Brazil stands out with a supply near 25 kg despite a GDP around $20,000, while Guinea-Bissau shows a supply of 20 kg with a much lower GDP near $4,000. The data is expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices to allow for comparison. The visible pattern places the highest vegetable oil supplies in North America and parts of South America, while many high-GDP European nations maintain moderate supply levels.
